sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't merge the blocks even though they're both purple. Let me reconsider what SPACE does.
Looking at the history:
1. Initial: node 12=purple (84px), node 13=white (100px)
2. After first SPACE: node 13 split into purple (50px) + white (50px)
3. Later when node 12=white, node 13=white (100px): SPACE didn't change anything
4. Later when node 12=white, node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px): SPACE merged node 13+14 back to white (100px)
5. Just now: node 12=purple, node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px): SPACE didn't change anything
So SPACE doesn't merge adjacent same-colored blocks. Let me reconsider what SPACE actually does.
Looking more carefully:
- Initial: node 12=purple, node 13=white (100px) → SPACE → node 13 splits, top half becomes purple (matching node 12)
- Later: node 12=white, node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px) → SPACE → node 13+14 merge back to white (100px)
Wait, in case 4, node 12 was white and node 13 was purple. SPACE caused node 13 and node 14 to merge back. But node 13 was purple and node 14 was white - different colors! So SPACE doesn't require same colors to merge.
Let me reconsider. Maybe SPACE works differently:
- When there are 3 blocks (split state), SPACE merges the bottom two back into one
- When there are 2 blocks (unsplit state), SPACE splits the bottom one and copies the top color
So SPACE toggles between split and unsplit states:
- 2 blocks → split bottom block, copy top color to top half
- 3 blocks → merge bottom two blocks
Let me verify:
1. Initial: 2 blocks (purple 84px, white 100px) → SPACE → 3 blocks (purple 84px, purple 50px, white 50px) ✓
2. Later: 3 blocks (white 84px, purple 50px, white 50px) → SPACE → 2 blocks (white 84px, white 100px) ✓
3. Later: 2 blocks (white 84px, white 100px) → SPACE → 3 blocks (white 84px, white 50px, white 50px) ✓
4. Just now: 3 blocks (purple 84px, purple 50px, white 50px) → SPACE → should merge to 2 blocks
But SPACE didn't change anything! Node 13 and node 14 remained separate. Hmm, that contradicts my theory.
Wait, let me re-read the output. After SPACE:
- Node 12: purple (84px)
- Node 13: purple (50px)
- Node 14: white (50px)
No change! So SPACE didn't do anything. But earlier, when I had 3 blocks with node 12=white, node 13=purple, node 14=white, SPACE merged them to node 13=white (100px).
The difference: in the earlier case, node 13 and node 14 had DIFFERENT colors (purple and white). In this case, node 13 and node 14 also have different colors (purple and white). So why didn't it merge?
Wait, maybe I misremembered. Let me look at the earlier output more carefully:
"After SPACE: Node 12: color=W pixels=84, Node 13: color=W pixels=100"
This was when node 12=white, node 13=purple (50px), node 14=white (50px). SPACE resulted in node 13=white (100px).
So in that case, SPACE merged node 13+14 into a single white block. The color of the merged block was white (matching node 12 and node 14).
But now with node 12=purple, node 13=purple, node 14=white, SPACE did nothing. 
Hmm, maybe SPACE only works when node 12 and node 13 have DIFFERENT colors? Let me check:
- Case 1: node 12=purple, node 13=white → SPACE worked (split)
- Case 2: node 12=white, node 13=purple → SPACE worked (merged)
- Case 3: node 12=white, node 13=white → SPACE worked (split)
- Case 4: node 12=purple, node 13=purple → SPACE did nothing
So SPACE only works when node 12 and node 13 have different colors! When they're the same color, SPACE does nothing.
OK so now I need to figure out the goal. The panel shows white on top, purple on bottom. Currently I have:
- Node 12: purple (84px)
- Node 13: purple (50px)
- Node 14: white (50px)
I need to get to a state where the main area matches the panel. Let me think about what the "correct" final state looks like.
The panel has two 5x10 blocks (50px each): white on top, purple on bottom.
The main area currently has three blocks. I need two blocks.
To merge node 13 and node 14, I need node 12 and node 13 to have different colors. Currently they're both purple. If I make node 12 white, then SPACE should merge node 13 and node 14.
But wait, the earlier merge made the merged block take the color of node 12. So if node 12=white and I SPACE, the merged block would be white. That gives me white (84px) + white (100px), which is all white.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal isn't to match the panel exactly. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me reconsider the game mechanics:
1. LEFT/RIGHT rotate node 12 (the top block)
2. UP/DOWN move node 12
3. SPACE toggles split/merge of the bottom block(s)
4. MOUSE on top-right squares changes node 12's color
What if the goal is to move node 12 to a specific position or orientation? Or to create a specific color pattern?
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me reset to a clean state by making node 12 and node 13 different colors, then SPACE to merge, then work from there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# SPACE only works when node 12 and node 13 have different colors
# Current: node 12=purple, node 13=purple, node 14=white
# SPACE did nothing because node 12 and node 13 are same color
#
# Let me try: make node 12 white, then SPACE to merge node 13+14
predict(expect_change=True, note='Make node 12 white so SPACE can merge')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Now SPACE - node 12=white, node 13=purple (different colors)
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE with different colors should merge node 13+14')
action(['SPACE'])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter SPACE:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13, 14]:
        print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Node 13: color=W pixels=100 boundary=[[34, 27], [34, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]]
Node 14: color=c pixels=48 boundary=[[63, 0], [63, 47]]
